snippets.title: A minimal binary tree node
snippets.code:
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class Node:
    key: int
    left: Optional['Node'] = None
    right: Optional['Node'] = None

#       4
#      / \
#     2   6
#    / \ / \
#   1  3 5  7
root = Node(4,
    Node(2, Node(1), Node(3)),
    Node(6, Node(5), Node(7)),
)
snippets.explanation: A node holds a key and two child pointers; the tree is just a reference to the root.

snippets.title: Recursive traversals
snippets.code:
def pre_order(n):
    if not n: return
    yield n.key
    yield from pre_order(n.left)
    yield from pre_order(n.right)

def in_order(n):
    if not n: return
    yield from in_order(n.left)
    yield n.key
    yield from in_order(n.right)

def post_order(n):
    if not n: return
    yield from post_order(n.left)
    yield from post_order(n.right)
    yield n.key

print(list(in_order(root)))   # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7] — sorted!
snippets.explanation: In-order on a BST always returns keys sorted — a useful sanity check that the BST invariant holds.

snippets.title: BST search and insert
snippets.explanation: At each step you drop one whole subtree — that's the O(log n) magic, *when balanced*.

snippets.title: Level-order (BFS) with a queue
snippets.explanation: Swap the queue for a stack and you get DFS — same skeleton, different ADT.

snippets.title: Use SortedDict for an actually-balanced ordered map
snippets.code:
from sortedcontainers import SortedDict

sd = SortedDict()
for k, v in [(7,'g'),(1,'a'),(4,'d'),(2,'b')]:
    sd[k] = v
print(list(sd))         # [1, 2, 4, 7]  — kept in sorted order
print(sd.irange(2, 5))  # range queries are O(log n)
snippets.explanation: SortedDict uses a balanced structure (sorted list of buckets) — O(log n) for the operations a vanilla BST risks getting wrong.
